items.headline: How I Really Rebuilt
items.angle: Mirror the competitor's raw, confessional structure. Pose the common question about how you rebuilt after losing everything, but instead of landing on 'grit,' reveal the real answer was having systems that couldn't be stolen.
hooks: People ask how I rebuilt after losing everything. The honest answer isn't very motivational.
I wish I could tell you I rebuilt with pure grit. I didn't.
In 2018, I went for a hike in Alaska. When I came back, my window was smashed and everything I owned was gone.
Everyone wants the motivational story about hitting rock bottom. This isn't it.
The most common question I get is how I kept going. It wasn't hustle.
talking_points: People always ask me how I kept going after I lost everything in Alaska back in 2018.
They expect some story about grit. About how I just outworked the problem and willed my way back.
To be honest with you, I don't have that story. I don't think pure hustle would have saved me.
When I came back to my truck that day, the window was smashed. My cameras, my lenses, my hard drives with all my work... gone.
Everything I used to do my job was gone in an instant. But the business itself was still running.
My client intake forms were still working. My email sequences were still sending. My project management system was still assigning tasks.
They could steal my tools. But they couldn't steal my systems.
That's when I learned the real lesson. Hustle is fragile. It depends on you feeling motivated. It depends on your car window not getting smashed.
A system doesn't care how you feel. It just works. It runs while you sleep, or while you're on the side of a road in Alaska figuring things out.
So if you want to build something that lasts, don't just rely on grit. Build systems that can't be taken away from you. That's what we teach in FieldWork.

They stole my gear, but they couldn't steal my systems. My automated client funnels were still running. My email sequences were still sending. The business kept breathing, even when I couldn't. That's the difference. Your motivation can disappear. Your grit can run out. But a system? A system runs no matter what. It works while you sleep, or while you're picking up the pieces. That's how you really rebuild. You don't just 'figure it out'. You build something that can't be broken in the first place.

The Story: My truck window was smashed in Alaska.
The Insight: They stole my gear, but not my systems.
The Philosophy: Systems are anti-fragile, hustle is not.
The Call to Action: Build systems that run without you.

items.headline: My Stupid 8-Figure Goal
items.angle: Christian should reframe the competitor's 'revenue goal' exercise into a 'time freedom' exercise. He'll expose the vanity of chasing huge revenue numbers that only buy more complexity, not more of the life you actually want to live offline.
hooks: For years, my goal was to build an 8-figure agency. I just realized that was a stupid goal.
I did an exercise that proved my dream life costs 79% less work than I thought.
Stop chasing a million dollars a month. Start chasing your 'Enough' number.
I calculated the cost of my perfect life. The number was surprisingly small.
My goal used to be more revenue. Now it's more time in a kayak. Here's the math.
talking_points: For the longest time, I had this goal of building a $10 million a year company. It sounded good. It sounded impressive. But I recently realized it was a pretty stupid goal.
So I did this exercise the other day. I calculated my 'FieldWork Number'. What's the exact revenue my business needs to generate for me to live my ideal life? Not just a good life. An incredible one.
On the list was a small cabin in Montana. Taking three full months off every year to be in the field. A dedicated fund for last-minute photography trips to Iceland or New Zealand.
It also included being able to fly my family out to join me wherever I am in the world, and hiring a small, killer team to run the systems so I don't have to.
All of that, while the business maintains healthy profit margins and I'm still saving and investing 50% of everything I take home.
The number? $2.1 million in revenue a year. That might sound like a lot. But it's nothing compared to the $10 million I was chasing.
Suddenly, there's almost $8 million of unnecessary revenue. That's not just money. That's $8 million worth of complexity, headaches, and time chained to a desk for no real gain in my life.
The moment I saw that number, the pressure was gone. I realized chasing the bigger number just meant building a bigger cage for myself. Trading my life for my ego.
Your business should be a system that buys back your time, not a monster that eats it. Anything beyond what you need to live your ideal life is just ego.

This is what we do in FieldWork. We find your number, then we install the AI and automation systems to hit it. So you can build online, and live offline. If you want help finding your number, just comment 'FIELDWORK' below.

items.headline: The Day I Lost Everything
items.angle: Frame the 'Alaska story' not just as a loss, but as the origin story for the entire 'systems beat hustle' philosophy. The trauma of losing every physical asset created an obsession with building digital, unbreakable systems.
hooks: In 2018, I went for a hike in Alaska. When I came back, everything I owned was gone.
I'm going to tell you about the worst day of my life. Because it explains everything about how I build businesses today.
I used to believe hard work was everything. A single car window taught me I was wrong.
A part of me is still terrified of losing it all again. Here's the system I built to fight that feeling.
This isn't a motivational story. It's about how a smashed window in a parking lot changed my entire life.
talking_points: I'm going to share something that I don't talk about a lot. It’s the origin of why I am so obsessed with systems. And maybe you’ll recognize the pattern in your own life.
In 2018, I spent four and a half months on a documentary project, driving from California to Alaska. My entire life was in my van.
One afternoon, I went for a hike. When I came back, my window was smashed. Everything was gone.
My cameras. My hard drives. Four months of work. Everything I had hustled for, just erased.

And in that silence, standing in that parking lot, I realized something. Hard work is fragile. Anything you build that depends on one location, one person, or one piece of glass can be taken from you in an instant.
That day created a pattern in me. An obsession with building things that can't be stolen. Things that aren't fragile.
You can steal my camera. You can't steal my customer acquisition system. You can smash my hard drive. You can't smash an AI employee that runs 24/7, booking calls while I sleep.
I stopped building things that required my hands and started building engines that run without me. That's the entire idea behind FieldWork.
It's about using AI and automation to build a business online, so you can live your life offline. Without the fear that one bad day can wipe it all out.
If you want to learn how to build a business that can't be smashed with a rock, comment the word 'FieldWork' below and I'll send you the details.

My hard drives with months of work. The physical result of all my hustle was just gone. And I realized hard work is fragile. Anything that depends on one piece of glass is a liability. That day, I became obsessed with building things that couldn't be stolen. You can steal a camera. You can't steal a customer acquisition system. You can smash a hard drive. You can't smash an AI employee that works while you sleep. I stopped building things and started building engines. That's the core of FieldWork.
